lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What, shall this speech be spoke for our excuse?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Or shall we on without apology?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: The date is out of such prolixity:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: We’ll have no Cupid hoodwink’d with a scarf,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Bearing a Tartar’s painted bow of lath,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Scaring the ladies like a crow-keeper;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Nor no without-book prologue, faintly spoke

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: After the prompter, for our entrance:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: But let them measure us by what they will;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: We’ll measure them a measure, and be gone.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Give me a torch: I am not for this ambling;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Being but heavy, I will bear the light.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Nay, gentle Romeo, we must have you dance.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Not I, believe me: you have dancing shoes

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: With nimble soles: I have a soul of lead

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: So stakes me to the ground I cannot move.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: You are a lover; borrow Cupid’s wings,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And soar with them above a common bound.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I am too sore enpierced with his shaft

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To soar with his light feathers, and so bound,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I cannot bound a pitch above dull woe:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Under love’s heavy burden do I sink.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And, to sink in it, should you burden love;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Too great oppression for a tender thing.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Is love a tender thing? it is too rough,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Too rude, too boisterous, and it pricks like thorn.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: If love be rough with you, be rough with love;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Prick love for pricking, and you beat love down.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Give me a case to put my visage in:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: A visor for a visor! what care I

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: What curious eye doth quote deformities?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Here are the beetle brows shall blush for me.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Come, knock and enter; and no sooner in,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: But every man betake him to his legs.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A torch for me: let wantons light of heart

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Tickle the senseless rushes with their heels,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: For I am proverb’d with a grandsire phrase;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I’ll be a candle-holder, and look on.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The game was ne’er so fair, and I am done.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Tut, dun’s the mouse, the constable’s own word:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: If thou art dun, we’ll draw thee from the mire

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Of this sir-reverence love, wherein thou stick’st

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Up to the ears. Come, we burn daylight, ho!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Nay, that’s not so.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: I mean, sir, in delay

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: We waste our lights in vain, like lamps by day.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Take our good meaning, for our judgment sits

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Five times in that ere once in our five wits.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And we mean well in going to this mask;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But ’tis no wit to go.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Why, may one ask?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I dream’d a dream to-night.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And so did I.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Well, what was yours?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: That dreamers often lie.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: In bed asleep, while they do dream things true.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: O, then, I see Queen Mab hath been with you.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: She is the fairies’ midwife, and she comes

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: In shape no bigger than an agate-stone

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: On the fore-finger of an alderman,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Drawn with a team of little atomies

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Athwart men’s noses as they lie asleep;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Her wagon-spokes made of long spinners’ legs,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: The cover of the wings of grasshoppers,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: The traces of the smallest spider’s web,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: The collars of the moonshine’s watery beams,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Her whip of cricket’s bone, the lash of film,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Her waggoner a small grey-coated gnat,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Not half so big as a round little worm

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Prick’d from the lazy finger of a maid;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Her chariot is an empty hazel-nut

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Made by the joiner squirrel or old grub,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Time out o’ mind the fairies’ coach-makers.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And in this state she gallops night by night

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Through lovers’ brains, and then they dream of love;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: O’er courtiers’ knees, that dream on court’sies straight,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: O’er lawyers’ fingers, who straight dream on fees,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: O’er ladies’ lips, who straight on kisses dream,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Which oft the angry Mab with blisters plagues,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Because their breaths with sweetmeats tainted are:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Sometime she gallops o’er a courtier’s nose,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And then dreams he of smelling out a suit;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And sometime comes she with a tithe-pig’s tail

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Tickling a parson’s nose as a’ lies asleep,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Then dreams he of another benefice:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Sometime she driveth o’er a soldier’s neck,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And then dreams he of cutting foreign throats,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Of breaches, ambuscadoes, Spanish blades,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Of healths five-fathom deep; and then anon

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Drums in his ear, at which he starts and wakes,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And being thus frighted swears a prayer or two

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And sleeps again. This is that very Mab

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: That plats the manes of horses in the night,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And bakes the elf-locks in foul sluttish hairs,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Which once untangled much misfortune bodes:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: This is the hag, when maids lie on their backs,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: That presses them and learns them first to bear,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Making them women of good carriage:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: This is she—

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Peace, peace, Mercutio, peace!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thou talk’st of nothing.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: True, I talk of dreams,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Which are the children of an idle brain,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Begot of nothing but vain fantasy,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Which is as thin of substance as the air

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And more inconstant than the wind, who wooes

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Even now the frozen bosom of the north,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And, being anger’d, puffs away from thence,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Turning his face to the dew-dropping south.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: This wind, you talk of, blows us from ourselves;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Supper is done, and we shall come too late.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I fear, too early: for my mind misgives

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Some consequence yet hanging in the stars

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Shall bitterly begin his fearful date

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: With this night’s revels and expire the term

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Of a despised life closed in my breast

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: By some vile forfeit of untimely death.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But He, that hath the steerage of my course,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Direct my sail! On, lusty gentlemen.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Strike, drum.

stage_directions.text: Enter Romeo, Mercutio, Benvolio, with five or six Maskers, Torch-bearers, and others.

glosses.definition: the conventional prologue maskers recited before entering a feast

glosses.phrase: The date is out of such prolixity
glosses.definition: such long-windedness is out of fashion
Cupid blindfolded — the traditional masque costume
a curved wooden toy bow in Tartar style — a stage-prop Cupid
a scarecrow; a boy paid to frighten birds — clumsy, unimpressive
memorized introduction; recited by rote

glosses.phrase: measure us by what they will
glosses.definition: judge us as they please
dance with them (pun: 'measure' as judgment and as stately dance)
light-footed dancing
sad; melancholy — weighted
dead-weight spirit; grief as gravity
the winged god of love's means of flight
painfully stuck with Cupid's arrow
spring above a certain height

glosses.phrase: case to put my visage in
glosses.definition: a mask to cover my face
a mask for a mask — Mercutio's self-mocking joke about his looks
note defects
heavy, jutting eyebrows — on the mask
start dancing
cheerful, frivolous people
the rushes strewn on the floor — inanimate, unfeeling
old man's saying — about sitting out the dance
spectator — proverbially the candle-holder watches the game

glosses.phrase: The game was ne’er so fair, and I am done
glosses.definition: proverb: when you are losing, quit while you are ahead
be quiet; keep still (proverb — dun-colored mice hide at night)
pull you out of the mud — from the game of 'dun is in the mire' where players pull a log free
saving your presence — apology for the crude word coming next
waste time — burning a candle in daylight is wasteful
the five mental faculties: common wit, imagination, fantasy, estimation, memory
fairy queen of dreams in English folklore — the midwife who delivers dreams to sleepers
the fairy who brings dreams to birth; also the fairies' own midwife
a small gem set in a finger-ring — Mab is that small
a city councilor; high-ranking burgher
tiny creatures; motes — the smallest imaginable beings
across
daddy-long-legs; spiders' legs
harness straps that attach animals to a carriage
the pale light of the moon, imagined as strap-material
gossamer; cobweb thread
driver of a cart

glosses.phrase: Prick’d from the lazy finger of a maid
glosses.definition: from a lazy girl's idle finger — tiny worms were believed to breed in the fingers of the slothful
the size of Mab's coach
squirrel-carpenter — as if the squirrel had crafted the shell
from time immemorial
curtsies; formal bows made at court
Mab punishes women with cold sores — folk-etiology of herpes
breath fouled by candy — folk belief that blisters came from bad breath
the courtier sniffing for favor — about to 'smell out' an opportunity
detecting a legal case or patronage opportunity — courtiers' livelihood
tail of a pig given as church tribute — tickles the parson into dreaming of more tithes
priest of a parish
a church living; salaried parish post
gaps blown in enemy walls
ambushes (Spanish military term)
Toledo swords — finest Renaissance steel
deep drinks drunk as toasts; five-fathom = 30 feet, hyperbolic
at once; suddenly
a military drum imagined in his dream
braids horses' manes — the folk-belief that fairies caused the tangles
tangled mats of hair — the marks left by fairy mischief
predicts bad luck
old witch — Mab in her darker aspect
lies on top of them — the incubus myth; Mab causes nocturnal emission
good bearing — with a bawdy pun on sexual bearing / capacity to bear a child
you're speaking of nothing — Romeo interrupts Mercutio's runaway flight
daydreaming mind; unoccupied thought
empty imagination
changeable; fickle
the south wind, bringer of rain and dew
distracts us; carries us away from our purpose
I have a foreboding; I fear

glosses.phrase: consequence yet hanging in the stars
glosses.definition: some destiny written in the stars, still pending
bring to an end the appointed time
premature death

glosses.phrase: He, that hath the steerage of my course
glosses.definition: God, the pilot of my life

annotations.title: The Queen Mab speech — performance, not summary
annotations.body: Mercutio's fifty-line flight is a showpiece of what he can do with language, not a paraphrasable argument. It starts miniaturist (fairy coach-spokes of spider-leg, hazel-nut chariot, atomies for drivers) and then accelerates from whimsy into disturbance: dreams of courtiers 'smelling out a suit,' lawyers' fees, lovers' kisses, ladies' blistered lips, and finally the hag who 'presses' maids 'and learns them first to bear.' The rhythm stretches and compacts unpredictably; the diction shifts from folkloric to medical to satiric; the speech reveals Mercutio as the play's most linguistically unstable character. Romeo interrupts: 'Peace, peace, Mercutio, peace! Thou talk'st of nothing.' But 'nothing' is the point — Mercutio has been talking about the 'vain fantasy' of dreams and, by extension, the self-deceiving fantasies of love, ambition, and revenge that drive the play. His virtuosity turns, at speech's end, into genuine bleakness.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)
Jonathan Bate, The Soul of the Age (2008)

annotations.title: Q1 vs. Q2 — the textual crux
annotations.body: Romeo and Juliet exists in two significantly different early texts. Q1 (1597, the so-called 'Bad Quarto') is shorter and is generally thought to be a memorial reconstruction, perhaps by actors. Q2 (1599) is longer, better-printed, and is the basis for modern editions (including Standard Ebooks, the source of this text). The Queen Mab speech is one of the places where Q1 and Q2 most visibly differ: Q1 gives a shorter, more restrained version of the speech, while Q2's text is the expansive virtuoso flight we have. Editors debate whether Q1 represents a pre-revision authorial draft or a performance-compressed acting text. Either way, the Q2 Queen Mab is the version that established Mercutio's reputation as Shakespeare's most verbally inventive minor character, and its length and escalation are intentional — the speech is designed to overflow, to reveal Mercutio's mind running out past his control.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)
René Weis, Arden Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2012)

annotations.title: Romeo's last speech before the ball — premonition as self-knowledge
annotations.body: Just before entering Capulet's house and meeting Juliet, Romeo speaks four lines of extraordinary prophetic weight: 'my mind misgives / Some consequence yet hanging in the stars / Shall bitterly begin his fearful date / With this night's revels.' He names the play's structure — the stars, the night's revels, the untimely death — but places himself in God's hands ('He that hath the steerage of my course'). Shakespeare lets Romeo speak with a self-knowledge he will immediately abandon. The premonition is not a plot-device; it is a kind of self-recognition the play asks us to notice. Romeo knows, at the door of the ball, what the prologue told us — and proceeds anyway. That is the shape of tragedy.

trials.prompt: Who, in English folklore, is Queen Mab?
options: The queen of Scotland during Shakespeare's lifetime
The fairies' midwife — she delivers dreams to sleepers
A famous Elizabethan prostitute
A character from Ovid's Metamorphoses

trials.prompt: Which best describes the rhetorical trajectory of Mercutio's Queen Mab speech?
options: Steady tone throughout — all whimsical
It begins miniaturist and whimsical, then accelerates into bitter satire and disturbing imagery (blisters, hags, the erotic hag pressing maids)
Purely descriptive throughout
Rhyming couplets from start to finish

trials.prompt: How does Romeo interrupt Mercutio's Mab speech, and what does it tell us?
options: 'Well spoken, friend!' — admiringly
'Peace, peace, Mercutio, peace! Thou talk'st of nothing' — sensing Mercutio has run past himself
He asks for the speech to continue
He says nothing at all

trials.prompt: What editorial fact does Q1 (1597) versus Q2 (1599) tell us about this scene?
options: The two quartos are identical
Q1 is a 'Bad Quarto' whose Queen Mab speech is significantly shorter than Q2's; Q2 is the basis for modern editions
Q2 is thought to be a pirated actors' memorial
Neither Q1 nor Q2 mentions Queen Mab

trials.prompt: What does Romeo feel as he approaches the Capulet ball?
options: Delight at the expected dancing
Premonition — 'my mind misgives / Some consequence yet hanging in the stars' — and yet he goes in anyway
Indifference to the evening
Anger at Tybalt
